planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil has warmed to at least 65°F. Scarify seeds by nicking the hard seed coat or soaking in warm water for 24 hours before planting to improve germination rates.

directions: Scarify hard seeds before sowing by gently nicking or sanding the seed coat, or soak overnight in warm water, to break dormancy and improve germination. Direct sow at or just after last frost into well-drained, poor-to-average soil — Partridge Pea thrives in lean conditions and will perform poorly in overly rich or wet soils. Thin seedlings to 12–18 inches apart; plants fix nitrogen through root nodules and will self-seed prolifically to naturalize an area over time. Bright yellow flowers from June through September attract pollinators, and the seed pods are an important food source for Northern Bobwhite, pheasants, and other wildlife.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost once soil is reliably warm. Scarify the hard seed coat or soak in warm water overnight before planting to break dormancy and encourage germination.

directions: Sow scarified seeds directly into well-drained, sandy or loamy soil after last frost when soil temperatures exceed 68°F. Sensitive Partridge Pea is a smaller, more compact annual than its close relative Chamaecrista fasciculata, making it well-suited to garden edges and disturbed or lean soils in warmer regions. The pinnate leaflets fold shut when touched, a thigmonastic response that can help identify the plant. Like all Chamaecrista species, it fixes atmospheric nitrogen through root nodules and self-seeds reliably, returning each season in established plantings.

planting.note: Best sown in fall for natural cold stratification over winter, or direct sow in early spring. Seeds may also be cold-stratified for 4–6 weeks in the refrigerator before spring sowing.

directions: Sow seeds in fall to allow natural cold stratification, or cold-stratify refrigerated seeds for 4–6 weeks before a spring direct sowing. Ruellia humilis is a low-growing native perennial that thrives in hot, dry conditions — it is excellent for road cuts, rock gardens, and dry slopes where many other plants struggle. The lavender-blue tubular flowers closely resemble petunias and appear throughout summer, providing nectar for hummingbirds and long-tongued bees. Note that this is the well-behaved native species and should not be confused with Ruellia simplex (Mexican Petunia), which is invasive in the Southeast; established plants spread modestly by self-seeding to form a colony.

planting.note: Sow in fall for natural cold stratification, or cold-stratify seeds for 4–6 weeks before direct sowing in early spring. Surface sow and lightly press into soil; seeds need light to germinate.

directions: Surface sow in fall or after a 4–6 week cold stratification in spring, pressing seeds lightly into the soil surface as they require light for germination. Rose Vervain is a low-spreading native perennial that produces abundant clusters of rose-pink to purple flowers from spring through fall, making it one of the longest-blooming native groundcovers available for hot, dry sites. It spreads by trailing stems that root at nodes and self-seeds to gradually colonize sunny, dry borders, rock gardens, and slopes. Deadheading spent flower clusters encourages continued bloom and reduces self-seeding if naturalization is not desired.

planting.note: Fall sow outdoors for natural cold stratification, or cold-stratify refrigerated seeds for 4–6 weeks before spring sowing. Surface sow as seeds need light exposure to germinate.

directions: Surface sow in fall for natural cold stratification, or cold-stratify seeds for 4–6 weeks in the refrigerator before spring sowing, pressing seeds gently into the soil surface since light aids germination. White Vervain is a tall, upright native perennial that thrives in partial shade and is well suited to woodland edges, shaded roadsides, and naturalized areas where it can reach 3–6 feet. The slender, branching stems bear tiny white flowers from June through October that are highly attractive to a range of specialist native bees, small butterflies, and other beneficial insects. Plants self-seed readily and can naturalize into loose colonies; allow some seed heads to mature in place if naturalization is desired.

planting.note: Fall sow outdoors for natural cold stratification over winter, or cold-stratify seeds in moist medium in the refrigerator for 4–6 weeks before spring sowing. Scarification of the hard seed coat may further improve germination.

directions: Scarify seeds and sow in fall for natural cold stratification, or cold-stratify in the refrigerator for 4–6 weeks before sowing in spring after last frost. Panicled Tick-Trefoil is a native perennial legume for woodland edges and shaded naturalized areas, where it fixes atmospheric nitrogen through root nodules and provides important habitat value. Small, pink pea-like flowers appear in summer and are pollinated by long-tongued bumblebees that sonicate the flowers; the triangular lobed seed pods that follow cling to fur and clothing for dispersal. Birds — particularly Wild Turkey and Northern Bobwhite — eat the seeds, making this an excellent addition to wildlife-focused native plantings.
